Theories: Entrepreneurial Ecosystem Theory, Resource Mobility Theory

Title: Navigating the Skies: The development of legitimacy of electrofuels in the aviation industry
Abstract/Summary: This thesis has examined the legitimacy of electrofuels as a sustainable fuel alternative in the aviation industry between 2017 and 2022. Since the aviation industry continues to be a hard-to-abate sector, which sees a worrisome continued growth in emissions, new fuel technologies are introduced to reduce emissions. Electrofuels are such a technological field that could supply aeroplanes with net-zero emission fuels. However, as this technological field is still in development, expectations and visions of electrofuels diverge. By applying theory on Technological Innovation Systems, this thesis has delivered an overview of innovation systems and legitimacy development by coding news articles and mobilising the Socio-Technical Configuration Analysis. The findings included actor and concept networks and are introduced through different development phases of the innovation system.
Main Research Question: How has the legitimacy of electrofuels developed as a sustainable fuel alternative in the aviation industry between 2017 and 2022?
Justification for SDG: The thesis addresses the need for sustainable fuel alternatives in the aviation industry to reduce emissions, aligning with the goal of combating climate change.
Keywords: electrofuels, aviation, legitimacy, Technological Innovation Systems, Socio-Technical Configuration Analysis

Title: Not Your Cup of Tea: Exploring Single-use Plastic Cup Behaviour among Dutch Students
Abstract/Summary: The excessive production and consumption of single-use plastic cups significantly impacts the environment and climate. In response, the Dutch government implemented a tax on disposable plastic cups and containers in July 2023 to reduce single-use plastic cup consumption. However, many people continue to use single-use plastic cups in their daily lives. This study examines the factors perpetuating the Students’ Paradox regarding single-use plastic cup consumption in the Netherlands. A literature review identified studies on the Theory of Planned Behaviour (TPB) and its relation to pro-environmental behaviour regarding single-use plastics. Subsequently, 16 students from different universities are interviewed on factors influencing their single-use plastic cup consumption. The results classified factors into extensive environmental awareness and limited pro-environmental behaviour, with the latter addressing the research question.
Main Research Question: What factors contribute to the limited pro-environmental behaviour regarding single-use plastic cup consumption among Dutch students?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the study addresses the consumption patterns of single-use plastic cups and aims to promote sustainable alternatives among students.
Keywords: single-use plastics, students, environmental awareness, pro-environmental behaviour, Theory of Planned Behaviour

Title: The Dutch farmer as boundary object: Media frames and roles of the Dutch farmer pertaining the nitrogen crisis
Abstract/Summary: The current way of producing food is harming nature, which is especially present in countries with a relatively big agricultural sector. The Netherlands is dealing with a nitrogen crisis, mainly because of the immense agricultural sector. A societal controversy is present on what the role of the farmer in this crisis is. The media frames the crisis which leads to the attribution of roles to the farmer and fuels the controversy even more. This study seeks to find the roles that are attributed to the farmer. This research used the framing analysis method proposed by Benford and Snow to analyze newspapers and find the role of the farmer in diagnostic and prognostic frames present in these newspapers. Three frames were found in which the farmer has a victim, protester or cause role.
Main Research Question: What roles are attributed to the Dutch farmer in the context of the nitrogen crisis as framed by the media?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the sustainability of food production and the implications of agricultural practices on the environment, which aligns with responsible consumption and production.
Keywords: Boundary object, Media framing, Roles, Nitrogen crisis, Netherlands, Sustainable transition

Title: Enhancing Healthcare Innovation through Coaching: An Analysis of the ZonMw IOC Subsidy Program
Abstract/Summary: The Dutch healthcare system is under pressure due to an aging population and a shortage of professionals. This study aims to analyze the impact of the ZonMw IOC subsidy program on healthcare innovation. A mixed-methods approach was employed, collecting quantitative data from 67 respondents through surveys and qualitative data from open-ended responses. Findings indicate that goal achievement and diverse coaching activities enhance value creation and learning ability, while acceptance by healthcare professionals and organizational readiness are crucial for positive outcomes. Recommendations for ZonMw include tailored coaching and improved communication strategies.
Main Research Question: How does the ZonMw IOC subsidy program influence the implementation and scaling of healthcare innovations?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on improving healthcare outcomes through innovation and effective coaching.

Title: Organisation and Governance of Energy Hubs on Dutch business parks: A multiple case study
Abstract/Summary: Energy hubs on business parks are expected to be a part of the solution for grid congestion in the Netherlands. This study aims to gain an understanding of the complex organisational and governance dynamics of energy hubs. This was explored by conducting a qualitative multiple case study approach. Data was collected through a combination of desk research and semi-structured interviews with 13 stakeholders. The study identified the key stakeholders and examined four most directly involved stakeholder groups regarding their roles, influence, interests, and challenges. Furthermore, the study proposes several solutions to address identified barriers, which can be used by policymakers and industry stakeholders to steer and stimulate energy hub development in the Netherlands.
Main Research Question: What are the organisational and governance dynamics of energy hubs on Dutch business parks?
Justification for SDG: The study focuses on energy hubs, which are integral to improving energy access and addressing grid congestion, aligning with the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: Energy hubs, Governance, stakeholders, business parks, grid congestion
Specific Methods: Qualitative multiple case study, Semi-structured interviews

Title: Site-specific differences in transition discourse: A comparative case study of the transition to urban cycling
Abstract/Summary: Cycling is considered a low-cost and sustainable mode of transportation for short distances within cities. Despite the ongoing transition in urban mobility, urban cycling and the influence of discourses on transitions are relatively under-researched. This thesis examines the discourse of urban cycling in two German cities. To this end, the transition developments and discourses were mapped over a period of 23 years by utilizing the MLP and MDD frameworks. This study suggests that due to the repeated correlations between discourse and transition, discourse likely influences transition (and vice versa).
Main Research Question: How do site-specific differences in transition discourse influence the transition to urban cycling in Osnabrück and Münster?
Justification for SDG: The thesis focuses on urban cycling as a sustainable mode of transportation, contributing to the development of sustainable urban mobility solutions.
Keywords: urban cycling, transition discourse, Osnabrück, Münster, Multi-Level Perspective, discourse analysis
Specific Methods: Discourse analysis, Semi-structured interviews, Case study

Title: Large Language Models in Dutch Healthcare
Abstract/Summary: Technological innovation may offer a solution for the urgent problem of surging healthcare expenditures in Dutch healthcare. With the arrival of ChatGPT, the focus on artificial intelligence, particularly on the utilization of Large Language Models (LLMs) in healthcare, intensified. This report uses the Technological Innovation System (TIS) analysis framework to examine its systemic attributes and the potential emergence of systemic strengths and weaknesses. Data was collected through desk research and semi-structured interviews. The results show that the Dutch Healthcare Large Language Model TIS is dependent on the properties of the Dutch Healthcare Artificial Intelligence TIS in most functions, highlighting the challenges and potential of LLMs in the healthcare sector.
Main Research Question: How can the Technological Innovation System framework be applied to assess the state of Large Language Models in Dutch healthcare?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on the application of technological innovations in healthcare, aiming to improve healthcare delivery and address rising costs.
Keywords: Large Language Models, Technological Innovation Systems, Dutch Healthcare, Artificial Intelligence

Title: The public perception of Precision Agriculture Technologies in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: The agricultural sector faces great sustainability challenges due to issues like soil degradation, water pollution, biodiversity loss, and climate change, hindering the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals. It is important to address these issues to balance sustainable agriculture with food security. To achieve this goal, solutions like intensifying sustainable agriculture via digital technologies (Agriculture 4.0) have emerged. One such technology is precision agriculture. Precision Agriculture Technologies (PATs) offer significant potential for enhancing sustainable agriculture by optimizing crop yields and fertilizer use. However, their adoption faces challenges due to potential drawbacks such as high investment, which is often overlooked when assuming universal adoption. This study aims to explore public perceptions of PATs in the Netherlands, essential for aligning technology with societal needs.
Main Research Question: How do the public perceptions of Precision Agriculture Technologies (PATs) in the Netherlands influence their adoption and integration within the agricultural system?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the study addresses the challenges of food security and sustainable agriculture through the adoption of Precision Agriculture Technologies.
Keywords: Precision Agriculture Technologies, public perception, sustainable agriculture, technology adoption
Theories: Klerkx et al.’s framework on agricultural digitalization

Title: The effects of multi-system dynamics on the diffusion of electrofuels
Abstract/Summary: While sustainability transition studies primarily analysed single socio-technical systems, this approach disregards the complexity of sustainability transitions that often involves actors, technologies, and institutions of multiple systems. Therefore, this thesis proposes an integral framework to understand better how multi-system dynamics affect the diffusion of innovations using: 1) institutional theory (the socio); 2) technological sector interactions (the technical); and 3) boundary-spanning and system-entangling activities of actors between multiple socio-technical systems. Following an abductive research design by iteratively analysing the acquired empirical data from 23 semi-structured interviews and 10 informal expert discussions using the qualitative analysis software of NVivo, a case study of the diffusion of electrofuels (e-fuels) into the maritime sector as one of the ‘hard-to-abate’ sectors is conducted to validate this novel approach. The results show that using these different theoretical concepts has uncovered the different natures and levels of socio-technical drivers and barriers for e-fuel adoption into the maritime sector stemming from multi-system interactions.
Main Research Question: How do multi-system dynamics affect the diffusion of electrofuels in the maritime sector?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on the diffusion of innovative electrofuels within the maritime sector, which is crucial for advancing sustainable industrial practices.
Keywords: electrofuels, maritime sector, Sustainability transitions, multi-system dynamics, socio-technical systems
Specific Methods: Semi-structured interviews, Expert interviews, Qualitative analysis

Title: Who should own the windy commons? Geopolitical and sustainability implications for Dutch offshore wind energy production ownership
Abstract/Summary: Geopolitical tensions and growing recognition of dependencies have sparked debates on the ownership of utilities. Offshore wind is promised to become a major part of the Dutch energy mix. Due to privatization, these generation assets are predominantly in the hands of major foreign energy companies. However, the shareholder distribution and the underlying investors remain opaque. Some would argue a lack of public ownership has put strain on the promotion of public values and the domestic retention of public capital. This leads to consider whether the current ownership configuration is optimal. Building on a theoretical foundation of nationalization and privatization debates, the present study sought to evaluate four ownership scenarios varying in their level of government involvement: Status Quo, Mixed Ownership, Nationalization, and Multilateral Cooperation. The scenarios were compared through an intuitive logics approach against a set of performance-indicating criteria, namely energy security, affordability, ecosystems, and innovativeness.
Main Research Question: What is the optimal ownership configuration for Dutch offshore wind energy production?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the ownership of offshore wind energy, which is crucial for achieving affordable and clean energy in the Netherlands.
Keywords: offshore wind energy, ownership, nationalization, privatization, energy security

Title: Transitions in the risk assessment regime: prospects from the case of animal-free testing in the cosmetics industry
Abstract/Summary: This thesis examines the transition from animal testing to animal-free methods in risk assessment in the cosmetics industry, focusing on the ban on animal testing in Europe. It explores how this transition affected the risk assessment regime, how actors responded, and the effect on innovation in the industry. The study utilizes desk research, Socio-Technical Configuration Analysis, and interviews with industry representatives to analyze regulatory changes and the context of the transition. The findings reveal that the Cosmetics regulation forbids animal tests, while REACH still requires them, highlighting the role of NGOs and industry incumbents in the transition process.
Main Research Question: How did the animal testing ban change the risk assessment regime in the cosmetics industry?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the transition to animal-free testing methods, promoting sustainable practices in the cosmetics industry and reducing reliance on animal testing.
Keywords: animal testing, animal-free methods, risk assessment, cosmetics industry, regulatory changes

Title: External Organizational Identity Change of Organizations in the Energy Sector A Case Study of Energy Suppliers in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: As climate change is a globally well-known problem, the importance of the energy transition becomes even more prevalent. Many studies have been performed on the energy transition before, investigating the energy sector as a whole, analyzing the success and failure of specific innovations. However, little attention is paid to the individual organizations that are implementing innovations during this transition period. This research uses a multiple case study of three Dutch organizations in the energy sector to explore the potential change in organizational identity. Results show that the organizations did not experience significant changes in identity, with Greenchoice being perceived as a green energy supplier, while Essent and Nuon/Vattenfall maintained a traditional identity despite a takeover.
Main Research Question: How does the organizational identity of energy suppliers in the Netherlands change during the energy transition?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on energy suppliers and their role in the energy transition, which directly relates to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: External Organizational Identity, Energy transition, Organizational Identity, Case Study, Energy Suppliers

Title: Towards a healthy food transition in the Netherlands: The case of third-level education catering
Abstract/Summary: This study addresses the increasing percentage of overweight Dutch students aged 18-25, primarily due to unhealthy dietary habits. It explores the role of campus caterers in shaping student eating behavior and identifies mismatches between the goals of caterers, educational organizations, and students. Using stakeholder interviews and a workshop, the research formulates three pathways for transitioning to a healthier food environment: changing student behavior, increasing student retention, and making healthy items more attractive. The findings highlight the importance of addressing pricing issues for healthy food options to ensure the success of these pathways.
Main Research Question: To what extent are there mismatches between the goals of third-level education caterers, the organizations they cater to, and the customer’s wants, and which pathways can be taken to transition to a healthier food environment?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the study focuses on improving the dietary habits and health of students, addressing the rising obesity rates among young adults in the Netherlands.
Keywords: Healthy Food Coalition, third-level education, catering, student health, dietary habits

Title: Technological Niches of Sustainable Aviation Fuel
Abstract/Summary: The continuously growing aviation sector is responsible for 7% of Dutch CO2 emissions and is one of the most difficult sectors to abate emissions. Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) is considered as a promising pathway to reduce these CO2 emissions. This thesis seeks to understand how and under what circumstances technological niches of SAF can reconfigure the regime of the Dutch socio-technical system of aviation fuel. Socio-Technical Configuration Analysis (STCA) was employed to study prevalent storylines and advocacy coalitions in three distinct phases of the public discourse of this sector. The analysis revealed that the successful breakthrough of the niches of second-generation biokerosene and synthetic kerosene depended on internal niche development processes and a de-stabilized regime through the occurrence of critical moments.
Main Research Question: How and under what circumstances can technological niches of Sustainable Aviation Fuel reconfigure the regime of the Dutch socio-technical system of aviation fuel?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the thesis addresses the reduction of CO2 emissions in the aviation sector through the development of Sustainable Aviation Fuel.
Keywords: Sustainable Aviation Fuel, socio-technical system, technological niches, aviation sector, CO2 emissions
Specific Methods: Socio-Technical Configuration Analysis, coding in NVivo, concept congruence networks, actor congruence networks
Theories: Strategic Niche Management, Advocacy Coalition Framework

Title: Overcoming the barriers of electrifying Dutch light hydrocarbon cracking A mission-specific innovation system analysis
Abstract/Summary: The Light Hydrocarbon Cracking (LHC) industry needs to decarbonise its production process to achieve the nationally set mission for a carbon-neutral industrial heat system by 2050. The main solution to this mission is electrification of the cracking process, also known as e-cracking. This study aimed to identify and analyse the barriers inhibiting this transition. A modified Mission-specific Innovation System (MIS) analysis was used to explore the barriers qualitatively. Expert interviews and desk research revealed seven central barriers, including financial risk incalculability and insufficient communication channels. Targeted recommendations were made to alleviate these barriers and improve the innovation system's performance.
Main Research Question: What are the barriers inhibiting the electrification of the Light Hydrocarbon Cracking industry and how can they be addressed?
Justification for SDG: This thesis addresses barriers to innovation in the LHC industry, which is crucial for developing sustainable industrial processes and infrastructure.
Theories: Mission-oriented Innovation Systems, Technological Innovation Systems

Title: Mind the gaps: navigating actors and issues in the regional innovation ecosystem
Abstract/Summary: The current trajectory for the Dutch agricultural sector is highly unsustainable, and change is urgent. The Foodvalley regional innovation ecosystem (RIES) could be instrumental in this transition. This research aims to better understand the role of transition intermediaries in addressing overlooked issues within the RIES. The governance gap approach is employed to understand the role of intermediary organisations. A mixed-methods design was used, including quantitative indicators and interviews with eighteen actors in Foodvalley. Collaborative governance gaps were identified, and a new interpretation of integrative governance gaps was suggested. Five types of governance gaps and four intermediary functions were identified, highlighting the importance of collaboration and integrative solutions.
Main Research Question: How do transition intermediaries address governance gaps in the Foodvalley regional innovation ecosystem?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on improving the sustainability of the agricultural sector, which is crucial for achieving food security.
Theories: Regional innovation ecosystem theory, Governance gap approach

Title: Enabling Circular Economy in African Economies: Identifying Skills for Circular Business Models in Start-Ups
Abstract/Summary: The Circular Economy (CE) offers a sustainable approach to resource management, but its implementation in African economies faces challenges. This study explores the skills required by circular start-ups (CSUs) in Africa to operate Circular Business Models (CBMs). While existing literature identifies the importance of skills in CE, there is a gap in empirical studies focusing on skill development for CBMs in Africa. Using an abductive research approach, this study employed qualitative methods through interviews with 30 CSUs across nine African countries. The research develops a skill taxonomy specific to CSUs operating across African economies by identifying needed skills and understanding external factors influencing them. Findings show that all skills from the baseline taxonomy are essential, with added emphasis on circular literacy and adaptability.
Main Research Question: What skills are required by circular start-ups in Africa to operate Circular Business Models?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on developing skills for circular business models, which are essential for promoting sustainable consumption and production patterns in African economies.
Keywords: Circular Economy, Circular Business Models, Circular Start-ups, Skills, Africa

Title: Beyond the Triple Aim Paradox: Unraveling Investor Influence in the Quintuple Aim Era
Abstract/Summary: Globally, healthcare systems face increasing pressure to deliver high-quality care while reducing costs to remain sustainable. This thesis explores whether investors influence a medical startup’s ability to achieve the Quintuple Aim. It examines the impact of investor’s finance/impact orientation on Quintuple Aim pursuit by medical startups using a quantitative approach. The findings reveal that impact-led investors do not consistently drive higher Quintuple Aim performance, and finance-first investors can also support positive outcomes. Investor diversity appears beneficial for Quintuple Aim outcomes, and both investor mixes dominated by impact-led or finance-first investors positively affect performance.
Main Research Question: To what extent do investors influence a medical startup’s ability to achieve the Quintuple Aim?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on improving healthcare systems and outcomes, which directly aligns with the goal of ensuring healthy lives and promoting well-being for all.
Keywords: Quintuple Aim, medical startups, investors, impact orientation, healthcare systems

Title: Virtual realities and categorical identity An analysis of categorical membership and user evaluation in the video game industry
Abstract/Summary: This thesis aimed to analyse the influence of VR technology on categorical membership and user evaluation within video games. As the video game industry is steadily growing, which is partly driven by the integration of VR technology, it is of interest to understand how VR technologies have shifted genre classifications and user expectations. Categorical membership refers to an organisation or its entity to be assigned to a specific category based on the exhibited characteristics shared within this category. Therefore, the following research question was composed: What is the influence of VR technologies on categorical membership and user evaluation within the video game industry? This study adopted a mixed method research design. The research design utilised a quantitative and a qualitative analysis of which utilised the quantitative analysis tested six hypotheses. This showed that VR video games tend to be classified under more genres, make use of less conventional genre combinations than in traditional gaming and receive lower user ratings as they are disruptive of nature.
Main Research Question: What is the influence of VR technologies on categorical membership and user evaluation within the video game industry?

Title: Stimulating solar energy in the Netherlands: Insights from Chinese solar energy policies and enterprise dynamics
Abstract/Summary: This thesis examines the influence of national innovation systems (NIS) on solar energy development in China and the Netherlands from 2013 to 2023. The research aims to identify factors contributing to the success of China's solar sector and the limitations faced by the Netherlands. A qualitative methodology, including literature analysis, policy document review, interviews, and comparative case studies, was employed. Findings reveal that China's centralized policies and government support have led to rapid innovation and cost reduction, positioning it as a global leader in solar technology. In contrast, the Netherlands, while excelling in solar power generation per capita, remains reliant on imported technology, hindering its competitive edge. The study offers insights for Dutch policymakers to enhance their solar industry.
Main Research Question: How did the national innovation systems of China and the Netherlands influence solar energy development between 2013 and 2023?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on enhancing solar energy development, which is crucial for achieving affordable and clean energy solutions.
Keywords: solar energy, national innovation system, China, Netherlands, Renewable Energy
Specific Methods: Literature review, Document analysis, Interviews, Comparative case study

Title: Responsible Innovation in Space Governance: A Study on Space Mining
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the governance of space mining, focusing on responsible innovation within the context of space policy. The aim is to analyze the evolving discourse surrounding space mining from 2015 to 2025, assessing both technological advancements and regulatory frameworks. A qualitative methodology is employed, utilizing document analysis of news articles, technical documents, and policy papers. The findings reveal a tension between economic growth and sustainability, highlighting the need for robust regulatory frameworks to ensure responsible innovation in the rapidly developing field of space mining.
Main Research Question: How can responsible innovation be integrated into the governance of space mining?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the need for innovative governance frameworks in the emerging industry of space mining, emphasizing the importance of responsible practices.
Keywords: Space governance, Responsible innovation, Space mining, Policy analysis

Title: Systematically uncovering transition dynamics: An STCA analysis of the Dutch energy system using LLMs
Abstract/Summary: This thesis addresses the gap in transition studies by exploring a novel methodological approach for analyzing socio-technical transitions. It employs Socio-Technical Configuration Analysis (STCA) enhanced by a Large Language Model (GPT-4o-mini) to automate the coding of 9,867 trade journal issues. The primary contribution is methodological, providing empirical insights into the Dutch energy system from 1880 to 1966. The findings reveal five systematic patterns in transition dynamics, supporting existing theories such as the Triple Helix model and the Multi-Level Perspective. This work advances both methodological tools and empirical understanding for guiding society towards sustainability.
Main Research Question: How can Socio-Technical Configuration Analysis (STCA) and Large Language Models be used to systematically identify long-term patterns of transitions in the Dutch energy system?
Justification for SDG: The thesis provides insights into the evolution of the Dutch energy system, which is crucial for understanding and promoting sustainable energy transitions.
Keywords: Socio-Technical Configuration Analysis, Large Language Model, Dutch energy system, transition dynamics
Theories: Triple Helix model, Multi-Level Perspective, Deep Transition framework

Title: Institutional dynamics and systemic intermediaries in the Dutch electrification transition: Navigating flexibility challenges
Abstract/Summary: This thesis examines how institutions and systemic intermediaries facilitate or hinder the development of flexibility in the Dutch electricity system. As electrification accelerates and grid congestion intensifies, flexibility is increasingly regarded as a prerequisite for reliable and sustainable system operation. However, the adoption of flexibility experiences drag, raising questions about the institutional and organisational dynamics at play. Using a qualitative case study approach, the research draws on ten semi-structured interviews and document analysis to examine how actors perceive their roles and responsibilities. The findings show that institutions create both enablers and barriers for flexible behaviour, complicating collective action and limiting systemic coordination.
Main Research Question: How do institutions and systemic intermediaries facilitate or hinder the development of flexibility in the Dutch electricity system?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on the flexibility challenges in the Dutch electricity system, which is essential for achieving reliable and sustainable energy transitions.
Keywords: flexibility, electricity system, institutional dynamics, systemic intermediaries, qualitative study

Title: Mapping Technological Sovereignty: A Geopolitical Network Approach to Domestic Capabilities and External Dependencies in Key Technologies
Abstract/Summary: Technological sovereignty refers to a nation's ability to act autonomously in securing its prosperity and safety through access to critical technologies. This study aims to develop a novel method for mapping a state's technological sovereignty by using patent citation networks to trace international knowledge flows. The method assesses technological sovereignty through five key indicators and is applied to the Netherlands and ten key technologies from the Dutch National Technology Strategy. Findings indicate that the Netherlands relies more on foreign knowledge than it contributes, with significant dependencies on OECD and NATO countries, particularly the United States. The methodology provides policymakers with a systematic tool for identifying vulnerabilities and opportunities.
Main Research Question: How can a state's technological sovereignty be effectively mapped using patent citation networks?
Justification for SDG: The study focuses on technological sovereignty and innovation capabilities, which are essential for building resilient industrial and technological infrastructures.
Keywords: Technological sovereignty, patent citation networks, geopolitical analysis, national capabilities, external dependencies
Specific Methods: Patent citation network analysis, data processing, indicator analysis

Title: Measuring Informal and Formal Knowledge Transfer Impact on Entrepreneurship Quantity for Healthcare Research Topics across EEs in the Netherlands.
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the impact of university research outputs, specifically academic publications and intellectual property (IP), on regional entrepreneurial activity across specific healthcare and life sciences (HCLS) research topics in the Netherlands. It distinguishes between informal (publications) and formal (patents) knowledge transfer (KT) mechanisms and examines how these are moderated by the quality of local entrepreneurial ecosystems (EEs). Using a topic modeling approach with Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), research topics were extracted from university outputs and matched with startup activities across regions in The Netherlands. Linear mixed-effect models reveal that both informal and formal KT channels positively correlate with entrepreneurial activity, with patents showing a stronger influence. However, EE quality has a nuanced role in that it positively moderates formal KT but diminishes the effect of informal KT.
Main Research Question: What is the effect of informal KT and formal KT on entrepreneurship for specific research topics across different EEs in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: The study focuses on the impact of university research and knowledge transfer on entrepreneurship, which is essential for fostering innovation and building resilient infrastructure in the healthcare sector.
Keywords: Knowledge Transfer, Entrepreneurship, Healthcare, Life Sciences, Entrepreneurial Ecosystems
Specific Methods: Latent Dirichlet Allocation, Linear mixed-effect models

Title: Exploring the causes of micro-level behavioural rebound effects in second-hand clothing consumption in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: The fashion industry faces increasing scrutiny for its environmental impact, while second-hand clothing (SHC) consumption is frequently promoted as a sustainable alternative to fast fashion. However, actual substitution of brand-new clothing (BNC) with SHC remains limited. This research investigates what causes insufficient substitution behaviour, leading to an increase in overall clothing consumption at a micro-level in the Netherlands. A qualitative study was conducted with 17 SHC consumers in Amsterdam, using the COM-B model as a framework. Findings show that substitution behaviour is mainly opportunity-driven, with availability, accessibility, and convenience acting as key drivers or barriers. Despite consumers’ sustainable intentions, their behaviour was inconsistent due to habits and social pressures.
Main Research Question: What causes insufficient substitution consumer behaviour at the micro-level of second-hand clothing consumption in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on sustainable consumption behaviour and the challenges in substituting new clothing purchases with second-hand options.
Keywords: second-hand clothing, consumption, Sustainability, COM-B model, behavioural patterns

Title: How equity funding shapes green start-ups success: Insights from Dutch start-ups
Abstract/Summary: This research investigates how equity funding influences the economic success of green start-ups in the Netherlands, focusing on the role of different investor types. Using a dataset of 968 Dutch start-ups, including 147 identified as obviously green, a two-step quantitative analysis was conducted. The findings reveal that green start-ups benefit more from strategic resources in addition to financial capital, confirming that while funding generally leads to economic success, the type of investor support significantly impacts growth. Specifically, green start-ups thrive with investors providing high levels of human resources, such as mentorship and strategic advice, highlighting the importance of tailored support in achieving sustainable business success.
Main Research Question: How do equity funding and investor types affect the economic success of green start-ups?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on green start-ups that contribute to sustainable industrial practices and innovation, aligning with the goal of promoting sustainable industries and fostering innovation.
Keywords: Green start-ups, Equity funding, Start-up growth, Corporate Venture Capital, Human Resources, Organisational Resource, Economic success, Investor types.

Title: Navigating Academia: A Scientometric Study of Early-Career Factors Influencing Academic Mobility
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the early-career factors influencing academic mobility through a scientometric approach. The aim is to identify key variables that affect the mobility of early-career researchers. A mixed methods research design is employed, combining quantitative data analysis with qualitative insights. The findings reveal that performance, funding, collaboration with top-tier researchers, and social constraints significantly impact academic mobility.
Main Research Question: What early-career factors influence academic mobility among researchers?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to understanding the dynamics of academic mobility, which is essential for enhancing educational quality and opportunities in academia.
Keywords: Mobility theory, Signalling theory, Early-Career Researcher signals
Specific Methods: Regression analysis, Principal Component Analysis, Case study

Title: Stakeholders’ Perceptions on a Just Transition towards Agricultural Robotization in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: To fulfill the rising global food demand, agricultural robotization is seen as a way to increase productivity, decrease pesticide use, and address labor shortages. Specifically, the Netherlands views automation and robotization as a possible solution. However, the transition towards robotization brings socioethical implications for decent work, society, and fairness. This study investigates how stakeholders perceive a just transition towards agricultural robotization in the Netherlands through 26 semi-structured interviews. The findings reveal that stakeholders perceive justice in two coherent ways: the economic-liberal and social-democratic approaches, highlighting the need for researchers to complement Responsible Research and Innovation approaches with Just Transitions theory.
Main Research Question: How do stakeholders perceive a just transition towards agricultural robotization in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the socioethical implications of agricultural robotization, focusing on decent work and fairness in the transition process.
Keywords: Just Transition, Agricultural Robotization, Stakeholders, Perceptions, Netherlands

Title: Towards Automated Literature-Based Innovation Output (ALBIO) Indicators
Abstract/Summary: This thesis develops and evaluates Automated Literature-Based Innovation Output (ALBIO), a modular, local pipeline that applies local large language models (LLMs) to extract, structure, enrich, and deduplicate innovation records from unstructured text. The study asks: How can LLMs automate and enhance LBIO while preserving data control and measurement quality? ALBIO is applied to 6,568 Dutch agricultural trade-journal articles (2015–2025) and is informally compared with SWINNO. Results show high precision (~81%) and moderate recall (~56%) on innovation identification, positioning ALBIO as a precision-first complement to manual LBIO. The approach counters biases of traditional innovation output indicators and broadens coverage beyond patents and surveys.
Main Research Question: How can LLMs automate and enhance LBIO while preserving data control and measurement quality?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on enhancing innovation measurement through automated methods, which aligns with the goal of fostering innovation and building resilient infrastructure.
Keywords: Automated Literature-Based Innovation Output, ALBIO, large language models, innovation measurement, Dutch agricultural sector
Specific Methods: Data collection, LLM setup, configuration, Process pipeline, Validation, deduplication

Title: Exploring the Viability of Dutch E-Methanol Production for the Maritime Sector A Strategic Niche and Multi-level Perspective on Timing, Barriers, and Breakthrough Potential
Abstract/Summary: As global pressure mounts to decarbonise the maritime sector, e-methanol, produced from green hydrogen and captured CO₂, has emerged as a promising alternative fuel. This thesis investigates methanol adoption and e-methanol production with the central question: How can Dutch e-methanol production with reused CO₂ reach a commercial and societal breakthrough in the maritime sector, what are the barriers, and how could these be overcome? Using Strategic Niche Management and the Multi-Level Perspective, the research analyses the interaction of e-methanol with the entrenched energy regime. The findings reveal that while technically feasible, significant barriers remain, including high production costs and unclear safety regulations.
Main Research Question: How can Dutch e-methanol production with reused CO₂ reach a commercial and societal breakthrough in the maritime sector, what are the barriers, and how could these be overcome?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on the decarbonisation of the maritime sector through the adoption of e-methanol, which directly contributes to climate action by promoting sustainable fuel alternatives.
Keywords: E-methanol, Maritime sector, Strategic Niche Management, Multi-Level Perspective, Decarbonisation
Specific Methods: Literature review, Expert interviews, Participatory observation, System mapping

Title: Transition towards sustainable space exploration: the case of the space debris industry
Abstract/Summary: This research explores the transition towards sustainable space exploration, focusing on the space debris industry. The aim is to understand the socio-technical characteristics and evolution of the space sector in light of increasing participation from various actors. The methodology involves a Socio-Technical-Network-Analyses (STNA) framework applied to a database of 289 articles discussing space debris from 2007 to 2019. The findings provide specific policy recommendations and insights into the legitimation processes within the space sector.
Main Research Question: How is the transition towards sustainable space exploration developing, and how can it be improved?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the issue of space debris, which is a significant concern for sustainable practices in space exploration, aligning with responsible consumption and production.

Title: Scientific knowledge on the Sustainable Development Goals
Supervisor: Dr. Gaston Heimeriks, Dr. Ed Noyons
Abstract/Summary: In 2015, the Sustainable Development Goals were introduced by the United Nations: 17 goals that aim at shared prosperity in a sustainable world for all. This study aims to explain the differences in SDG related knowledge bases among countries. The SDG related knowledge base of a country is established and several methods for collecting and mapping SDG research are analyzed. Findings show that there is not a ‘best’ method, but rather that all methods have advantages and disadvantages. The influence of a country’s knowledge base characteristics on its SDG research is analyzed using regression models. The findings indicate large differences between SDGs, with relatedness density positively affecting the SDG research share.
Main Research Question: How does the existing knowledge base of a country influence its SDG related research and achievement?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the study focuses on the collaboration and knowledge sharing necessary for achieving the Sustainable Development Goals across different countries.
Keywords: Sustainable Development Goals, knowledge base, SDG research, regression models, evolutionary economic geography
Specific Methods: Regression analysis, project analysis, comparative analysis

Title: Explaining Path Creation: An agency approach in regarding the path creation of artificial intelligence in the federal state of Saarland, Germany
Abstract/Summary: This research explores how and why regions develop along specific paths, focusing on the path creation of artificial intelligence in Saarland, Germany. The aim is to understand the role of agency in this process, utilizing the trinity of change framework. Through 25 semi-structured interviews, the study identifies three key episodes in the path creation process: increased collaboration among companies, fostering regional start-up development, and informing companies about AI advantages. The findings highlight the importance of actor interactions and the necessity of support from various actor groups for successful path creation.
Main Research Question: How do the actions of actors influence the path creation process of artificial intelligence in Saarland, Germany?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on the development of artificial intelligence, which is a key area of innovation and infrastructure in the region.
Keywords: path creation, artificial intelligence, Saarland, agency, regional development

Title: Air Travellers’ Willingness to Pay for a Sustainable Offer: A Discrete Choice Experiment
Abstract/Summary: Growing air travel demand requires for new sustainable solutions to be embraced by all players of the industry, including individual travellers. Due to the low adoption rates recorded for current voluntary carbon offset solutions, the level of success of sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) among travellers is difficult to predict, despite its potential to reduce up to 80% CO emissions. Insights on 563 Dutch individuals’ preferences were obtained from a survey, by integrating a Discrete Choice Experiment into the Motivation-Opportunity-Ability model. Results show that a sustainable option’s price is the most influential factor for its adoption. We argue that the most influential factors contributing to the increase in probability SAF adoption should be considered when marketing a SAF product in order to trigger behavioral change.
Main Research Question: What factors influence air travellers’ willingness to pay for sustainable aviation fuel?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the need for sustainable solutions in air travel, which directly relates to climate action and reducing carbon emissions.
Keywords: discrete choice experiment, MOA model, sustainable aviation fuel, behavioral change
Specific Methods: Discrete Choice Experiment, Conditional Logit Model

Title: Institutional Strategies in Technological Innovation Systems The Case of Decentralized Sanitation Solutions in eThekwini and the City of Cape Town
Abstract/Summary: Only half of the South African households in informal settlements have access to sanitation services. Decentralized sanitation solutions such as urine diversion toilets or communal ablution blocks are considered the only feasible alternatives to increase sanitation access in South Africa due to economic and topological conditions. However, these solutions are met by many protests as it is intertwined with inequality for a large number of citizens. Therefore, the initial social acceptance of these solutions is low. This thesis illustrates that the combination of the TIS and institutional work approach allows scholars and policymakers to better understand the actors’ individual actions and the influence of these actions on TIS performance.
Main Research Question: How can one municipality better overcome low social acceptance of decentralized sanitation solutions than another municipality in South Africa?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the critical issue of sanitation access in South Africa, focusing on decentralized solutions that can improve sanitation services for informal settlements.
Keywords: Decentralized sanitation, Technological Innovation Systems, Institutional work, Social acceptance, eThekwini, City of Cape Town
Organizations Studied: eThekwini Municipality, City of Cape Town

Title: Why do SMEs hire gig workers? – A survey of the Dutch hotel and manufacturing industry
Abstract/Summary: The gig economy is a new type of labour market, enabling the hiring of service providers for a one-time job through an online platform. In this research, I investigate in which circumstances companies hire this new type of worker – rather than employing workers in-house or hiring them externally through traditional channels. By developing a theoretical framework bridging the literature on transaction cost theory, human resources literature and literature on trust, I propose seven hypotheses on factors which potentially influence the hiring of gig workers. The findings show that benevolence is of significant influence, favouring internal staff as opposed to gig workers.
Main Research Question: In which circumstances do companies hire gig workers rather than employing workers in-house or hiring them externally through traditional channels?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the dynamics of the gig economy and its implications for employment practices, contributing to the understanding of decent work opportunities.
Theories: Transaction Cost Theory, Human Resources Literature, Trust Literature

Title: The influence of resource acquisition timing on nascent venture performance
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates how the timing of resource acquisition influences the performance of nascent ventures, specifically focusing on creation speed and growth. The research employs multiple linear regression analysis on data from the 'Perfect Timing' database, which includes over 800 nascent ventures in the ICT and alternative energy sectors. The findings reveal that the timing of human and capital resource acquisition significantly affects creation speed, while revenue is primarily influenced by the sector of operation. This highlights the importance of considering resource acquisition timing in understanding venture performance.
Main Research Question: How does the timing of resource acquisition influence nascent venture creation speed and growth?
Justification for SDG: This study contributes to understanding the factors that enhance nascent venture performance, which is essential for fostering economic growth and job creation.
Geographical scope: Germany, Italy, Netherlands, United States, United Kingdom
Keywords: resource acquisition, nascent ventures, performance, creation speed, growth
Country: Germany; Italy; Netherlands; United States; United Kingdom

Title: Exploring collaboration success factors for intermediary organizations in public-private partnerships
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the collaboration success factors for intermediary organizations within public-private partnerships in the European traffic management sector. The aim is to identify key elements that contribute to successful partnerships. A qualitative empirical research methodology was employed, utilizing case studies of various traffic management initiatives. The findings reveal critical success factors such as joint decision-making, clear goals, and strong partner interactions that enhance collaboration effectiveness.
Main Research Question: What are the collaboration success factors for intermediary organizations in public-private partnerships in the European traffic management sector?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on improving infrastructure and fostering innovation through effective public-private partnerships, aligning with the goals of SDG 9.
Keywords: public-private partnerships, traffic management, collaboration, success factors, intermediary organizations
Organizations Studied: ONTF Amsterdam, ONTF Antwerp, Smart Destination Amsterdam, Smart Destination Copenhagen, Roadworks Amsterdam, Roadworks Antwerp, Roadworks Munich

Title: Collaboration in the Circular Economy in the Netherlands: A Proximity Approach
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the hindering factors for a Circular Economy in the Netherlands, focusing on the necessity of collaboration among organizations. The research employs the EUPRO database and utilizes social network analysis and count regressions to analyze collaborations based on proximity dimensions. Findings reveal that organizations tend to collaborate with those having higher geographical, institutional, and cognitive proximity, which paradoxically may hinder collaboration success. The study concludes with policy recommendations to enhance collaborative efforts in the transition towards a Circular Economy.
Main Research Question: What drives collaborations in the circular economy in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the need for sustainable practices in waste management and resource utilization, aligning with the goal of promoting responsible consumption and production patterns.
Keywords: Circular Economy, Collaboration, Proximity, Social Network Analysis, Policy Recommendations

Title: Legitimation of unwanted, dispersed, hidden innovations
Abstract/Summary: The goal of this research was to identify which factors contribute to the legitimation of unwanted, dispersed, hidden innovations by evasive, dispersed, hidden institutional entrepreneurs in the Netherlands. Entrepreneurs that work with psychedelic substances are chosen as unit of analysis, because they work with this type of innovations. Those entrepreneurs were asked about their legitimation strategies and also outsiders of the sector were interviewed to examine how they view these legitimation strategies. After analyzing the interviews, a new theoretical model was formed explaining the pathway how entrepreneurs legitimize unwanted, dispersed, hidden innovations.
Flag: Missing Research Question, Missing SDG, Abstract too short

Title: The acceleration of small wins to contribute to the problem of plastic pollution
Author(s): Sanne Andreas Maria Joseph Veronica Bours
Abstract/Summary: In recent years, the accumulation of plastic pollution has been observed. The wickedness that is involved in this societal problem requires a suitable governance strategy. One literature stream that deals with wickedness from a bottom-up perspective is ‘small wins’. Small wins focus on incremental change to achieve sustainable result, through guidance of a mission or vision. This research aims to contribute to current literature with an empirical case to identify current small wins and propelling mechanisms in the plastic pollution problem. A qualitative research strategy was followed, involving a case study of sixteen initiatives that contribute to ‘removal measures’ for plastic pollution in Dutch waterways. The analysis shows that all propelling mechanisms from literature are important in accelerating small wins.
Main Research Question: How do propelling mechanisms amplify small wins related to the plastic pollution problem in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on addressing plastic pollution, which is a significant issue related to sustainable consumption and production patterns.
Keywords: plastic pollution, small wins, propelling mechanisms, wicked problems, mission-oriented innovation policy

Title: Age-friendly housing: navigating through a complex multi-regime environment
Abstract/Summary: Demographic change poses significant challenges for housing and healthcare, that deems current practices as uncontainable. Reforms in long-term care resulted in the closure of institutional residences, like nursing and care homes, which made new forms of housing including care services - necessary. Age-friendly housing as an envisioned solution for older adults to live at home independently as long as possible, can facilitate a transition of these sectors. This study developed an integrated framework capturing the dynamics of three sectors: housing, healthcare and ICT by exploring local initiatives and its system interactions. A qualitative approach in form of a multi-case design performing semi-structured interviews is conducted. The analysis identifies various configurations of actors and strategies, contributing to the understanding of the multi-system approach in age-friendly housing.
Main Research Question: How can age-friendly housing solutions facilitate the transition of housing, healthcare, and ICT sectors in response to demographic change?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the need for age-friendly housing solutions that enable older adults to live independently, contributing to sustainable urban development.
Keywords: Age-friendly housing, demographic change, multi-system approach, healthcare, ICT

Title: Experimentation in Mission-oriented Innovation policy: natural gas free districts
Abstract/Summary: This thesis focuses on the contribution of district level experiments to the Dutch national mission of the transition towards a natural gas free built environment in the Netherlands. In Mission-oriented Innovation Policy, using experiments is emphasized, but a clear description of the desired contribution of these experiments or guidelines to design such an experiment are lacking. Therefore, it is studied which factors contribute to the internal success and external success of experiments that are dedicated to this Dutch mission. Twelve experiments that are part of four different experimental programs are studied through interviews and analysis of local reports, newspaper articles, and governmental reports. It is found that factors stimulating different types of success may contradict each other, leading to specific policy recommendations.
Main Research Question: Which factors contribute to the internal and external success of experiments dedicated to the Dutch mission of a natural gas free built environment?
Justification for SDG: The thesis addresses the transition towards a natural gas free built environment, which directly relates to creating sustainable urban environments.

Title: Shaping the emerging alternative service regime from a Technological Innovation System perspective: the case of decentralized water and sanitation in Bangalore
Abstract/Summary: Caused by the rapid urbanization of the city, Bangalore has been lacking to provide adequate water and sanitation services for its citizens. This study aims to analyze the mechanisms contributing to the development and diffusion of decentralized water and sanitation technologies in Bangalore using the Technological Innovation System framework. A qualitative event analysis was conducted over a period of 15 years. Findings indicate that the (mis)alignment between shared visions, regulatory measures, and user legitimation has contributed to the (un)sustainable development of decentralized solutions. Integration of the identified sub-TISs could lead to a functional alternative service regime.
Main Research Question: How can the development and alignment of decentralized water and sanitation solutions in Bangalore be understood through the Technological Innovation System framework?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the study focuses on improving water and sanitation services in Bangalore through decentralized solutions.
Keywords: decentralized water, sanitation, Technological Innovation Systems, Bangalore

Title: Guiding discovery How knowledge development and innovation strategies are directed by the regional context
Abstract/Summary: Regional policy makers struggle in designing effective bottom-up and mission-oriented innovation policies that are embedded in their territorial context. Knowledge development has proven to be fundamental in these innovation dynamics and is said to be evolving in relation to the regional knowledge capabilities and institutional context. Therefore, this study aims to find how specific attributes of the regional knowledge base and institutional context relate to knowledge development in European regions and how these dynamics shape a region’s prioritisation strategy in the RIS3 program of the European Union. A series of quantitative analyses showed that the related diversification opportunities provided by the regional knowledge base, quality of government and institutional thickness have a positive relation to complex knowledge development.
Main Research Question: How do specific attributes of the regional knowledge base and institutional context relate to knowledge development in European regions?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the study focuses on innovation policies and knowledge development within regional contexts, which are essential for fostering industrial and infrastructural advancements.

Title: In search of a future meaning of man-made meat
Abstract/Summary: Cultured meat (CM) is an innovation that regards the promising production procedure of meat through cell-culturing in a laboratory, rather than through livestock farming. In the past, its development had strong governmental support in the Netherlands, however, its market introduction promptly stagnated due to the absence of fitting regulations. This research questions how frames influence the image and prospects of CM among policymakers and CM entrepreneurs by investigating the occurrence of frames from the webpages of Dutch CM entrepreneurs and the Dutch second chamber debates of policymakers. It is found that CM entrepreneurs and policymakers frame CM differently, with entrepreneurs focusing on its transformative capacity while policymakers emphasize its market value.
Main Research Question: How do frames influence the image and prospects of cultured meat among policymakers and cultured meat entrepreneurs?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the production and consumption of meat alternatives, which is crucial for sustainable food systems and reducing environmental impacts.
Keywords: Cultured meat, framing, policymakers, entrepreneurs, regulations

Title: Cloud computing technology in manufacturing firms
Abstract/Summary: Nowadays, digital technologies are transforming societal and business environments. One of these technologies, cloud computing technology, has gained much popularity and attention in recent years, and is about to radically reshape the manufacturing industry. This research aimed to increase knowledge on the process around cloud computing adoption, implementation, and usage in Dutch manufacturing firms. More specifically, adoption factors, challenges, and corresponding solutions were identified. For this purpose, an inductive and qualitative research approach was pursued. Twenty semi-structured interviews were conducted with employees from nineteen different manufacturing firms and one firm that helps to solve issues related to information technology within the manufacturing sector.
Main Research Question: What are the adoption factors, challenges, and corresponding solutions for cloud computing technology in Dutch manufacturing firms?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on the adoption and implementation of cloud computing technology, which is essential for enhancing innovation and efficiency in the manufacturing sector.
Keywords: cloud computing, manufacturing, adoption factors, challenges, solutions
Theories: Unified Theory of Acceptance, Use of Technology

Title: FROM THE MAN ON THE MOON, TO SPACE FOR THE MANKIND: A technological innovation system analysis of how the Copernicus earth observation infrastructure leveraged new applications in the Dutch water management sector
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the Copernicus Programme, an earth observation infrastructure developed by the European Space Agency and the European Commission, focusing on its application in the Dutch water management sector. The aim is to identify success factors and barriers affecting the diffusion of Copernicus applications. A qualitative case study was conducted, utilizing desk research and interviews analyzed through coding. The findings indicate a shift in innovation policies towards a mission-oriented approach, yet highlight challenges such as governmental dependency and legitimacy issues that hinder application diffusion. Recommendations for innovation policies are provided to enhance the program's effectiveness.
Main Research Question: What are the success factors and blocking mechanisms that stimulate or hamper the diffusion of the Copernicus applications in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the challenges posed by climate change and the potential of Copernicus data to provide solutions in the water management sector, aligning with the goals of climate action.
Keywords: Copernicus, earth observation, Water management, innovation policy, Technological Innovation Systems
Theories: Technological Innovation Systems, Mission-oriented Innovation Systems

Title: Path Creation in the Dutch’ Age Friendly Housing Domain
Abstract/Summary: The continent of Europe is facing major demographic changes due to an aging population, leading to challenges in healthcare and housing services. This thesis explores how entrepreneurs develop and commercialize technologies for active aging by participating in Living Labs. A qualitative inductive research approach was used, involving interviews with living lab coordinators and entrepreneurs. The study identifies different types of living labs in the Dutch elderly care sector and highlights the benefits for entrepreneurs. It concludes that while living labs have potential for socio-technical transitions, improvements are needed in various areas.
Main Research Question: How do entrepreneurs, as creators of new socio-technological pathways, use living labs to develop and commercialize technologies for active, healthy, and independent aging?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research addresses the challenges of an aging population and the need for innovative healthcare solutions to support healthy aging.
Keywords: Living labs, Path creation, Aging population, Entrepreneurs, Socio-technical transitions
Specific Methods: Interviews with living lab coordinators, Interviews with entrepreneurs

Title: A research portfolio analysis of the collaborative research networks of public-private partnerships for neglected tropical diseases
Abstract/Summary: Public-private partnerships (PPPs) play a crucial role in addressing neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) by uniting diverse stakeholders to tackle market failures. This research aims to analyze how the heterogeneous characteristics of PPPs influence their research portfolios and collaborative networks. Employing a mixed-methods approach, data was collected from PPP websites and the Web of Science, focusing on publication counts and diversity metrics. Findings indicate that broad disease scopes and mixed funding correlate with higher publication counts, while self-funding and larger network sizes enhance diversity in collaborative networks. This study contributes to the understanding of PPP dynamics in NTD research and offers insights for optimizing their strategies.
Main Research Question: How do the heterogeneous characteristics of public-private partnerships in the field of neglected tropical diseases affect their research portfolios in general and the collaborative research networks in particular?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the goal of ensuring healthy lives and promoting well-being for all by clarifying the role of PPPs in advancing research and access to treatments for neglected tropical diseases.
Keywords: public-private partnerships, neglected tropical diseases, research portfolio analysis, collaborative networks, diversity
Specific Methods: Research portfolio analysis, Quantitative analysis, Qualitative analysis
Organizations Studied: Drugs for Neglected Diseases Initiative, Novartis Institute for Tropical Diseases, The Special Program for Research, Training in Tropical Disease, Infectious Disease Research Institute, Global Program to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis

Title: Competitive Balance Through Diversity and Technological Ambidexterity: A Case Study of Magic: The Gathering
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the innovation mechanisms within the trading card game Magic: The Gathering (MTG), focusing on competitive balance, diversity, and technological ambidexterity. The aim is to analyze how these concepts influence the game's ability to remain competitive over time. A quantitative case study approach is employed, utilizing longitudinal data from MTG tournaments. The findings reveal that the strategy of changing the mixture of technical characteristics positively affects competitive balance, while other strategies show limited significance. This research contributes to understanding how games can inform innovation studies and offers insights applicable to various domains.
Main Research Question: What is the effect of the strategies that are implemented to preserve competitive balance over generations in Magic: The Gathering?
Justification for SDG: The research highlights the importance of innovation mechanisms in maintaining competitive balance within the game, which aligns with the goal of fostering innovation and sustainable industrialization.
Keywords: Magic: The Gathering, competitive balance, diversity, technological ambidexterity, Innovation

Title: Waiting games in healthcare Towards a predictive, preventive, personalized and participatory (P4) healthcare in the future
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the phenomenon of waiting games in healthcare, aiming to identify factors that contribute to these delays and propose solutions for a more efficient system. The objective is to develop a predictive, preventive, personalized, and participatory healthcare model. The methodology includes desk research, semi-structured interviews, and a dialogue workshop to gather insights from various stakeholders. The findings reveal multiple factors causing waiting games, such as infrastructure issues and regulatory challenges, and suggest scenarios to mitigate these delays.
Main Research Question: What factors contribute to waiting games in healthcare and how can they be overcome?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the thesis addresses healthcare efficiency and aims to improve patient outcomes by reducing waiting times.
Keywords: waiting games, healthcare, predictive, preventive, personalized, participatory
Specific Methods: Desk research, Semi-structured interviews, Dialogue workshop

Title: Green Growth in the Technology Space – Regional Diversification Pathways in Europe
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores green growth as a means to balance economic and environmental benefits, focusing on the development of regional capabilities to manage complex situations. It addresses the need to investigate the pathways towards green growth in various European regions, particularly catch-up regions that have successfully diversified their technological capabilities. A quantitative-exploratory approach was employed, utilizing green patent data to create a green fitness ranking of regions. The findings reveal patterns of regional development and suggest mechanisms for successful path development, emphasizing the importance of leveraging advanced technologies and related diversification opportunities.
Main Research Question: Which European regions have successfully diversified into more complex green technological capabilities over time? What are the similarities and differences between catch-up regions with regards to their green growth diversification pathways in the technology space?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on technological capabilities and innovation pathways in regions, aligning with the goal of fostering innovation and building resilient infrastructure.
Keywords: Green growth, Technological capabilities, Regional innovation, Diversification pathways, Green patents
Specific Methods: Green patent data analysis, Green fitness ranking, Technological relatedness mapping

Title: Governance through Goals: Influences on Goal Characteristics in Dutch Innovation Policies
Abstract/Summary: The grand challenges of the 21st century require changes in innovation policies and governance. This research focuses on 'governance through goals' perspectives, which are goal-setting policies aimed at resolving societal challenges. The study investigates how goal formulation can be effective and how formulation processes impact innovation policies. By analyzing three Dutch examples of goal-setting policies, this research identifies four goal characteristics: balance between ambition and achievability, enacting stakeholders, societal desirability, and directionality. The findings reveal the complex web of influences on these characteristics and emphasize the importance of well-designed formulation processes for effective goal-setting policies.
Main Research Question: How did goal formulation processes influence the goal characteristics of three Dutch goal-setting policies?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on innovation policies aimed at addressing societal challenges through effective goal formulation and stakeholder engagement.
Keywords: Governance, goal formulation, innovation policy, societal challenges, Netherlands
Organizations Studied: Ministry of Infrastructure and Water Management

Title: Non-profit Organizations as Developers and Drivers of Innovation: An Exploration of the Googly-eyed Garbage Gobbler
Abstract/Summary: This research explores the role of non-profit organizations (NPOs) as innovation developers, focusing on the Waterfront Partnership of Baltimore (WPB) in the environmental water clean-up technology sector. The study aims to understand how WPB drives innovation and what distinguishes it from other entrepreneurial actors. Through documentation reviews and interviews, the research identifies WPB's unique contributions to the innovation system, including filling gaps left by public and private actors, leveraging NPO advantages, and fostering collaborations. The findings highlight the potential of NPOs in strengthening innovation systems and provide insights for organizations interested in innovation.
Main Research Question: How was WPB able to drive innovation within the environmental water clean-up technological sector, and what can we learn about NPOs’ innovation system strengthening potential in general?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on environmental water clean-up, directly addressing the issue of marine pollution and the role of NPOs in promoting sustainable practices.
Keywords: non-profit organizations, Innovation, environmental technology, water clean-up, collaboration
Organizations Studied: Waterfront Partnership of Baltimore, Clearwater Mills

Title: Service Innovation in the Retail Sector
Abstract/Summary: Fast and complex changes in the retail landscape cause retailers to struggle and some even to close their doors. To survive, brick-and-mortar retailers are innovating their services to create a memorable customer experience. Understanding the innovation decision process is complex since the service innovation options are plentiful and retailers are known to have an unstructured approach to innovation. This research uses prior conditions from Rogers’ innovation-decision model to select factors previously identified in both retail and service innovation literature. Qualitative analysis of interviews with representatives from fashion retailers’ headquarters in the Netherlands shows that previous practice and felt needs barely influence the decision process. Results show that typical innovations are not complex and do not have a large impact on the service, are part of the omnichannel trend, and concern innovations in the collection.
Main Research Question: How do retailers make decisions in a turbulent retail landscape?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on service innovation within the retail sector, which is crucial for enhancing industry practices and fostering innovation.
Keywords: Innovation – decision-making - service innovation – service blueprint - retail – fashion – synthesis perspective – prior conditions
Specific Methods: Qualitative analysis, service blueprinting, Qualitative Comparative Analysis

Title: Air Travellers’ Willingness to Pay for a Sustainable Offer: A Discrete Choice Experiment
Abstract/Summary: Growing air travel demand requires for new sustainable solutions to be embraced by all players of the industry, including individual travellers. Due to the low adoption rates recorded for current voluntary carbon offset solutions, the level of success of sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) among travellers is difficult to predict, despite its potential to reduce up to 80% CO emissions. Insights on 563 Dutch individuals’ preferences were obtained from a survey, by integrating a Discrete Choice Experiment into the Motivation-Opportunity-Ability model. Results show that a sustainable option’s price is the most influential factor for its adoption. We argue that the most influential factors contributing to the increase in probability SAF adoption should be considered when marketing a SAF product in order to trigger behavioral change.
Main Research Question: What factors influence air travellers’ willingness to pay for sustainable aviation fuel?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the adoption of sustainable aviation fuel, which directly contributes to reducing carbon emissions in air travel, aligning with the goal of combating climate change.
Keywords: discrete choice experiment, MOA model, sustainable aviation fuel, behavioral change
Specific Methods: Discrete Choice Experiment, Conditional Logit Model

Title: The development of DESAR technologies in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the development of Decentralized Energy and Resource Recovery (DESAR) technologies in the Netherlands. The aim is to analyze the technological change and the emergence of new industries related to these technologies. A qualitative empirical research methodology is employed, utilizing case studies and interviews to gather data.
